elements.sent-2411-004: Mookerjea \inst{3 \institute{Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Calgary, Calgary, Canada \and Institut de Recherche en Astrophysique et Plan\'etologie, UniversitĂ© de Toulouse, CNRS, UPS, CNES, Toulouse, France \and Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics, Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, Mumbai 400005, India.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Star-forming regions host a large and evolving suite of molecular species.
elements.sent-2411-006: Molecular transition lines, particularly of complex molecules, can reveal the physical and dynamical environment of star formation.
elements.sent-2411-007: We use a multi-component local thermodynamic equilibrium model to determine the large-scale physical environment near DR21(OH) and the surrounding dense clumps.
elements.sent-2411-008: We follow up with a radiative transfer code for CH$_3$OH to look at non-LTE behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-009: We then use a gas-grain chemical model to understand the formation routes of these molecules in their observed environments.
elements.sent-2411-010: We find a warm and cold component each towards the dusty condensations MM1 and MM2, and a fifth broad, outflow component.
elements.sent-2411-011: We also reveal warm and cold components towards other dense clumps in our maps: N40, N36, N41, N38, and N48.
elements.sent-2411-012: We find thermal mechanisms are adequate to produce the observed abundances of H$_2$CO and CH$_3$CCH while non-thermal mechanisms are needed to produce CH$_3$OH.
elements.sent-2411-013: This method allows us to disentangle the different velocity and temperature components within our clump-scale beam, a scale that links a star-forming core to its parent cloud.
elements.sent-2411-014: We find numerous warm, $\sim$20-80~K components corresponding to known cores and outflows in the region.
elements.sent-2411-015: We determine the production routes of these species to be dominated by grain chemistry.
elements.sent-2411-016: The dynamic evolution of star formation leads to the production of a diverse suite of molecules.
elements.sent-2411-017: Molecular emission, then, reveals great detail about the physical and chemical conditions from which these species arise.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, wide-band mm and sub-mm observations are capable of capturing numerous rotational transition lines which are powerful diagnostics of their environment due to the different excitation conditions each line is sensitive to.
elements.sent-2411-019: In early-stage protostellar sources in particular, when the accreting protostar is still enshrouded in an envelope, there are two categories of molecules distinguished for their diagnostic capabilities: complex organic molecules (COMs) and carbon-chain molecules (CCMs).
elements.sent-2411-020: These species are sensitive to their environment, and are valuable tracers alongside more common, simple species such as CO, HCN, or HCO$^+$.
elements.sent-2411-021: COMs, carbon-bearing saturated complex molecules, are abundant in the hot (T $>$ 100 K) and dense ($n_{\text{H _2}$ $>$ 10$^6$ cm$^{-3}$) cores of star-forming regions (\citealt{blake1987, caselli1993, Cazaux2003, Ceccarelli2004 ; see \citealt{herbst2009 for a review of these molecules).}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: CCMs, on the other hand, are unsaturated hydrocarbons (e.g. C$_n$H, HC$_n$N) that are well-known in cold (T $\le$ 10 K) molecular clouds and starless cores (\citealt{avery1976, broten1978, kroto1978, little1978, sakai2010 ).}
elements.sent-2411-023: They are now also known to form in the warm gas enveloping protostellar systems in a process dubbed warm carbon chain chemistry (WCCC; \citealt{sakai2008, aikawa2008, sakai2013 ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: COMs and CCMs, due to this chemical differentiation, can help classify protostellar systems.
elements.sent-2411-025: And while these species have been used to detail star-forming regions, especially hot cores, high-mass star-forming regions (HMSFR), which are capable of producing stars with M $>$ 8 M$_\odot$, are more difficult to study due to their relative rarity, clustered environments, and quick evolutionary timelines.
elements.sent-2411-026: As the evolutionary processes of HMSFR are not fully understood, observing their molecular emission is a valuable tool to study these regions.
elements.sent-2411-027: The first HMSFR found with evidence for WCCC was DR21(OH) \citep{mookerjea2012 , a nearby and prominent region within Cygnus X.}
elements.sent-2411-028: DR21(OH) lies 2$\arcmin$ north of the DR21 H\,{ II region at a distance of 1.5~kpc \citep{rygl2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Its peak, known as DR21(OH)M or N44, has a total mass of about 10$^3$ M$_\odot$ and density of $n_{\text{H _2}$ 10$^6$~cm$^{-3}$ and is further resolved into two sources, MM1 and MM2 (\citealt{woody1989, mangum1991, motte2007 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-030: MM1 shows the stronger continuum emission at millimeter wavelengths \citep{liechti1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Both star-forming cores MM1 and MM2 are thought to be very young, with no visible H\,{ II region and only very weak continuum emission at the centimeter wavelengths \citep{argon2000 , i.e. in a pre-UCH\,{ II region phase.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Dust continuum observations suggest that MM1 is the brighter source (L = 1.7$\times$10$^4$ L$_\odot$; consistent with a B0V star) and shows evidence of star formation, whereas MM2 (L = 1.2$\times$10$^3$ L$_\odot$; early B star), though more massive, is fainter and most likely at an even earlier stage of evolution.
elements.sent-2411-033: These sources have been further differentiated: \citet{zapata2012 found nine mm sources between MM1 (SMA 5-9) and MM2 (SMA 1-4) each of mass 8-24 M$_\odot$; \citet{minh2012 identified two hot subcores in mm observations of MM1 corresponding to SMA 6 and 7.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Methanol masers (Class I, pumped collisionally) along a bipolar outflow contribute to the evidence of DR21(OH)M as a region of active star formation (\citealt{plambeck1990, kogan1998, kurtz2004, araya2009 ).}
elements.sent-2411-035: Near DR21(OH)M are several other dense clumps: DR21(OH)S (or N48), DR21(OH)W (N38), DR21(OH)N1 (N41), DR21(OH)N2 (N40), and N36 (named by \citealt{mangum1991, chandler1993, motte2007 ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: These sources exist along the DR21 ridge, consisting of filamentary structures that influence collapse (\citealt{schneider2010, hennemann2012 ).}
elements.sent-2411-037: N48, N38, and N40 are massive IR-quiet cores noted as possible precursors to high-mass protostars â€” in \citet{motte2007 the lack of IR emission suggests they are not forming a star yet, but SiO emission is indicative an outflow is being powered by a protostellar object.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In total, these sources make up a parsec-scale region with a mass of 4900 M$_\odot$ (based on 1.2 mm continuum emission, \cite{schneider2010 ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Interferometric observations have catalogued DR21(OH)M as a rich molecular source including hydrocarbons and COMs (\citealt{zapata2012, minh2012, girart2013, OrozcoAguilera2019 ), while observations of the larger scale environment detail the continuum emission and that of simple species such as CO, CS, SiO, N$^2$H$^+$, and HCO$^+$ (\citealt{richardson1994, lai2003, motte2007, schneider2010 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-040: We aim to link the molecular environment of DR21(OH)M at core scales to the larger clump and filament it resides in.
elements.sent-2411-041: We will use single-dish observations to map CH$_3$CCH as a CCM tracer as well as CH$_3$OH and H$_2$CO as COM tracers, and use an LTE model to determine the physical parameters of different gaseous components within the DR21(OH) region.
elements.sent-2411-042: The rest of the paper is structured as follows: Section~\ref{section:observations shows the observations and the data set used, Sect.~\ref{section:ltemodel includes the local thermodynamic equilibrium (LTE) modelling results and analysis, Sect.~\ref{section:discussion presents a chemical evolution model and discusses the findings, and Sect.~\ref{section:summary provides a brief summary.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We observed DR21(OH) in multiple frequency ranges with the IRAM 30-m telescope and the 100-m Green Bank Telescope (GBT).
elements.sent-2411-044: A detailed summary of these observations is found in~\citet{freeman2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: IRAM 30-m observations were completed November 2020 and April 2021 (project codes 021-20 and 122-20, PIs S.. Bottinelli and R.
elements.sent-2411-046: Using the on-the-fly observational mode with position switching, we mapped a $1' \times 1.5'$ region in DR21(OH) simultaneously in the frequency ranges 131.2 - 138.98~GHz and 287.22 - 295~GHz.
elements.sent-2411-047: The offset position was -120$\arcsec$ horizontally and -240$\arcsec$ vertically.
elements.sent-2411-049: Data reduction, and the production of maps, was completed with GILDAS/CLASS\footnote{https://www.iram.fr/IRAMFR/GILDAS .}
elements.sent-2411-050: At 291~GHz, the spectral channel width is 781~kHz or 0.80~km~s$^{-1}$, the beam size is 9.3$\arcsec$, and the pixel size is 4.4$\arcsec$.
elements.sent-2411-051: At 135~GHz, the spectral channel width is 391~kHz or 0.88~km~s$^{-1}$, the beam size is 20.5$\arcsec$, and the pixel size is 9.7$\arcsec$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Given the larger beam size, the 135 GHz data will not be discussed in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-053: GBT observations were completed in March 2021, in the frequency ranges 84.5-85.75~GHz and 95.55-96.8~GHz using all 16 beams of the ARGUS focal plane array and the VErsatile GBT Astronomical Spectrometer (VEGAS) spectral line backend (project code 21A-039, PI P.. Freeman).
elements.sent-2411-054: We obtained 1$\arcmin$ DAISY on-the-fly maps for both frequency ranges, centered as above, utilizing position-switching for the reference measurements.
elements.sent-2411-055: The spectral sampling with spectrometer mode 2 is 92~kHz in both frequency ranges, or 0.32~km s$^{-1}$ at 85~GHz and 0.28~km s$^{-1}$ at 96~GHz.
elements.sent-2411-056: The beam size is 9.2$\arcsec$ at 96~GHz and 10.0$\arcsec$ at 85~GHz, both with a pixel size of 2.0$\arcsec$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The data were reduced and calibrated using GBTIDL\footnote{https://gbtidl.nrao.edu/index.shtml .}
elements.sent-2411-058: In Sect.~\ref{section:ltemodel , the data are presented in units of $T_\mathrm{A ^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The LTE model, computed by definition in $T_\mathrm{mb}$ is converted to $T_\mathrm{A ^*$ using the telescope $B_\mathrm{eff}$/$F_\mathrm{eff}$ values provided by the respective observatories: GBT at 85 GHz, 0.4545; GBT at 96 GHz, 0.3838; IRAM EMIR at 290 GHz, 0.547.}
elements.sent-2411-060: In these data sets we detect numerous lines of CH$_3$CCH, a CCM tracer, and of CH$_3$OH and H$_2$CO, to be used as COM tracers.
elements.sent-2411-061: The identification of these lines is presented in Freeman et al. (submitted), and was done with CASSIS\footnote{https://cassis.irap.omp.eu/ ~\citep{vastel2015 using the Cologne Database for Molecular Spectroscopy\footnote{https://cdms.astro.uni-koeln.de/classic/ (CDMS) catalogue~\citep{MULLER2005 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Propyne, or methyl acetylene, CH$_3$CCH, is a symmetric rotor with many transitions closely spaced in frequency.
elements.sent-2411-063: Numerous lines are thus captured in a small bandwidth, and are useful for analysis of the physical conditions, especially temperature, in LTE (\citealt{irvine1981, Askne1984, kuiper1984, Bergin1994 ).}
elements.sent-2411-064: It has long been detected in low- and high-mass star-forming regions and is used as a tracer of chemical complexity (\citealt{snyder1973, lovas1976, Cazaux2003, taniguchi2018b, gianetti2017, santos2022 ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Methanol, CH$_3$OH, is the simplest alcohol molecule and an asymmetric top molecule \citep{ball1970 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: It has numerous transitions in the mm and sub-mm range which are abundant and commonly used as probes of the physical environment.
elements.sent-2411-067: H$_2$CO was the first polyatomic organic molecule detected in space \citep{Snyder1969 , and while not technically a complex species it is chemically associated with larger organic molecules.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Both H$_2$CO and CH$_3$OH are formed by the successive hydrogenation of CO on dust grain surfaces, and are precursor molecules to larger COMs (\citealt{Charnley1997, garrod2013 ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: These two species are detected across interstellar space: in cold clouds, hot cores, outflows, shocks, the Galactic centre, and external galaxies (see \citealt{herbst2009 and references therein).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.6]{figures/dr21oh_mom0_subplots_scale.png} \caption{Integrated intensity maps for select transition lines of CH$_3$CCH, H$_2$CO, and CH$_3$OH. The dense clumps as identified in \cite{motte2007} are shown in stars, with the main clump N44 in black (see also Fig.~\ref{fig:dr21regions}).} \label{fig:intintensity}
elements.sent-2411-070: Table~\ref{tab:lines lists the transition lines seen in our frequency ranges, with certain conditions based on the sensitivity of our observations.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We limit the scope of lines: for CH$_3$CCH an $A_{\mathrm{ij }$ $>$ 1$\times10^{-6}$ s$^{-1}$ and E$_{\mathrm{up }$ $<$ 200~K (to account for the a-CH$_3$CCH line that has E$_{\mathrm{up }$ $<$ 150~K, but has a higher tabulated value when the a- and e- types are combined in CDMS); for H$_2$CO an $A_{\mathrm{ij }$ $>$ 1$\times10^{-6}$ s$^{-1}$ and E$_{\mathrm{up }$ $<$ 150 K; for CH$_3$OH an $A_{\mathrm{ij }$ $>$ 2$\times 10^{-6}$ s$^{-1}$ (to remove the 84.5~GHz maser line) and E$_{\mathrm{up }$ $<$ 150 K.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Integrated intensity maps of select lines covering a range of upper energy levels are displayed in Figure~\ref{fig:intintensity .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Class I masers are often found in young star-forming regions (see \citealt{Ladeyschikov2019 ), including in DR21(OH) (\citealt{slysh1997, araya2009 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-074: In particular, the CH$_3$OH line at 84.5 GHz is a known maser in DR21(OH) (\citealt{batrla1988 ).}
elements.sent-2411-075: These masers, which are collisionally pumped, are unlikely to be well reproduced by LTE.
elements.sent-2411-076: Thus, to be careful with our LTE analysis, we removed this line.
elements.sent-2411-077: An LTE model was used to determine the physical parameters of the observed region -- size, line width, column density, excitation temperature, and source velocity.
elements.sent-2411-078: In LTE, we assume the excitation temperature represents the gas temperature.
elements.sent-2411-079: We use a python-based LTE model based on the CASSIS formalism\footnote{http://cassis.irap.omp.eu/docs/RadiativeTransfer.pdf (previously described in \citealt{freeman2023 for a similar analysis of AFGL 2591 and IRAS 20126+4104).}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The LTE model identifies all molecular transitions for one or more species and simultaneously fits these lines defined by the physical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-081: The resulting fit is the combination of parameters that best reproduces the spectral line profile as determined by a least-squares minimisation (represented as a reduced $\chi^2$ value).
elements.sent-2411-082: In the model, we use the Cologne Database for Molecular Spectroscopy\footnote{https://cdms.astro.uni-koeln.de/classic/ (CDMS; \citealt{MULLER2005 ) tags for each species.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The species types -- e.g. a- and e-CH$_3$CCH, o- and p-$c$-C$_3$H$_2$, A- and E-CH$_3$OH, o- and p-H$_2$CO -- were not differentiated as we do not detect enough lines of each.
elements.sent-2411-084: In the simplest case, an observed spectral line will arise from one physical gas component modeled by a single Gaussian profile with a centroid velocity ($v_\mathrm{LSR}$), line width ($\Delta v_\mathrm{FWHM}$), and brightness ($T_\mathrm{A}$*).
elements.sent-2411-085: However, in the case where the telescope's beam encompasses multiple physical components, the observed spectra may have to be modeled using multiple Gaussian profiles.
elements.sent-2411-086: We treated each species, CH$_3$CCH, CH$_3$OH, and H$_2$CO, separately in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-087: To determine the components of each species, we started with a one-component fit and looped the model over all pixels in the map.
elements.sent-2411-088: Where a one-component fit was not adequate, either by a visual inspection of the spectral line profile or by the resulting $\chi^2$ analysis, we added components as needed.
elements.sent-2411-089: This divided the map into regions described by their own, distinct, physical components.
elements.sent-2411-090: The regions, marked in Fig.~\ref{fig:dr21regions , correspond to the clumps found in \cite{motte2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: We gained an idea of what components were necessary based on (a) taking an example spectrum and fitting multiple Gaussians in CASSIS to find velocity and line width information, and (b) knowledge from the literature on what velocity and temperature components we expect to see at this scale.
elements.sent-2411-092: The results for these fits are presented in Table~\ref{tab:ltemodel .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.6]{figures/dr21oh_mom0_regions_0.png} \caption{The regions isolated for LTE modelling near and in DR21(OH), marked in white boxes over an integrated intensity map of the 12~K CH$_3$CCH $5_0$-$4_0$ line. From the top, the regions include: N40, N36, and N41 (labelled N); N44; N38; N48. The dense clumps, as designated by \cite{motte2007}, are marked by stars.} \label{fig:dr21regions}

The cores SMA 7 in MM1 and SMA 3 in MM2 are marked by x-es, and the clump of N44 is marked by a star (as designated in \cite{zapata2012} and \cite{motte2007}). CH$_3$OH 5 is moved to align with H$_2$CO 3, the broad components.} \label{fig:n44ntot}

elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[ width=0.5\textwidth]{figures/dr21oh_params_ntot_n38_1.png} \caption{Same as Fig.~\ref{fig:n48ntot} for the clump N38.} \label{fig:n38ntot}

elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{figures/dr21oh_params_ntot_n36_1.png} \caption{Same as Fig.~\ref{fig:n48ntot} for the N region, containing the clumps N40, N41, and N36.} \label{fig:n36ntot}
elements.sent-2411-175: In comparison to our other clumps, we also see weaker emission in this region (Fig.~\ref{fig:intintensity ).}
elements.sent-2411-176: The components distinguished by our LTE model show the large-scale environment around many, known core-scale features.
elements.sent-2411-177: DR21(OH), within the larger DR21 ridge, is a young complex with embedded protostars that have not yet disrupted the parent cloud.
elements.sent-2411-178: In order to determine the possible origin of these molecules in the observed environments, we modelled the species abundances using the NAUTILUS gas-grain code \citep{ruaud2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: We did this for all species in all components of DR21(OH), or N44 (we do not use the N44 designation for each component in this section, as all are N44).
elements.sent-2411-180: NAUTILUS uses three phases for chemical evolution -- gas, grain surface, and grain mantle -- and stimulates the abundances over time as a function of different physical conditions -- gas density, dust and gas temperature, visual extinction, ultraviolet flux and cosmic ray ionisation.
elements.sent-2411-181: In the code, coupled differential equations describe a network of over 10,000 reactions connecting 489 species.
elements.sent-2411-182: Before starting the model, we found the fractional abundances, or the ratio of the column density for a given species and the column density of molecular hydrogen, H$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-183: For our species, we used the modelled column density (Sect.~\ref{sect:n44 ) at its peak value.}
elements.sent-2411-184: We found the H$_2$ column density at the same position by re-gridding and smoothing the \cite{cao2022 H$_2$ map in CASA\footnote{https://casa.nrao.edu/ onto our grid and beam size.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Studies of the H$_2$ to $A_V$ conversion range from $A_V$ = $\frac{N_{H_2 {2.1\times10^{21 }$ (\citealt{rachford2009, zhu2017 ) to $A_V$ = $\frac{N_{H_2 {1.9\times10^{21 }$ (\citealt{bohlin1978, whittet1981 ).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: In this paper, we assumed an `average' value of $A_V$ = $\frac{N_{H_2 {2\times10^{21 }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: We used a standard cosmic ray ionisation rate of $1.3\times10^{-17}$ s$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-188: For the UV field, the NAUTILUS code provided a function of the form $S\times10^8$~photons~cm$^{-2}$~s$^{-1}$ \citep{ruaud2016 where S is a scaling factor.}
elements.sent-2411-189: We used a scaling factor of 1 to model a general interstellar radiation field.
elements.sent-2411-190: We note that this could be higher in the DR21 environment, however, given the large H$_2$ column densities and resulting values of $A_V$, larger UV field strengths are unlikely to have any effect on the chemistry due to shielding.
elements.sent-2411-191: We used the volume densities, $n_{\mathrm{p-H_2 }$, similar to the CH$_3$OH RADEX models.}
elements.sent-2411-192: We matched the other species components as: CH$_3$OH components 1 and 2 at 1 $\times$ 10$^{8}$ to H$_2$CO components 1 and 2; CH$_3$OH Component 5 at 5 $\times$ 10$^6$ to H$_2$CO component 3; CH$_3$OH components 3 and 4 at 2 $\times$ 10$^7$ to CH$_3$CCH components 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-193: This comparison is based on the temperature, line width, and velocity parameters found for each species (Table~\ref{tab:ltemodel ).}
elements.sent-2411-194: Such high densities are not unusual for a dense core (e.g. \citealt{mookerjea2012, girart2013 ) and are representative of (near) LTE conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-195: We started with a two-phase model in NAUTILUS: a cold quiescent cloud followed by a warm-up phase powered by embedded protostars.
elements.sent-2411-196: The cold cloud had initial abundances representing the diffuse ISM, a temperature of 10~K, a density of $10^4$~cm$^{-3}$, a visual extinction of 50 magnitudes, and it evolved for 10$^5$ years (see \citealt{ruaud2016 ).}
elements.sent-2411-197: Then, in the warm-up phase the gas and dust temperature is the observed temperature of each pixel found from the LTE model, and the visual extinction is calculated from the average H$_2$ column density.
elements.sent-2411-199: For all three components of H$_2$CO, the warm-up phase was adequate to reproduce our observed gas-phase abundances (Fig.~\ref{fig:2stage , top).}
elements.sent-2411-200: For components 1 and 2, it takes just over 100 years to produce the observed abundances, while for component 3, the assumed outflow, it takes over 10$^3$ years for the abundances to match.
elements.sent-2411-201: The same chemistry applies to all components, on different timescales -- at the time the abundances match, the domination production mechanism changes (Figs.~\ref{fig:proddesth2co1 , \ref{fig:proddesth2co3 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-202: The main production mechanisms at the start are the gas phase reactions: \begin{equation}\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{CH_3} \rightarrow \mathrm{H} + \mathrm{H_2CO}, \label{eq:h2co}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\mathrm{CH} + \mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow \mathrm{H} + \mathrm{H_2CO}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-203: Then, these are replaced by desorption of H$_2$CO off the grain surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-204: For all components, the destruction mechanism follows a similar timeline.
elements.sent-2411-205: H$_2$CO is, at first, solely destroyed by the gas phase reaction: \begin{equation}\mathrm{H_2CO} + \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_2} + \mathrm{CO},\end{equation} and after 100 or 10$^3$ years, depending on the component, the dominant destruction mechanism becomes adsorption onto the grain surface.
elements.sent-2411-206: At this point, the abundance of gas phase C has sharply decreased.
elements.sent-2411-207: Overall, the temperatures of $\sim$80~K are enough to produce the observed abundances.
elements.sent-2411-208: The binding energy of H$_2$CO is reported as 3260~K, which corresponds to a desorption temperature of 110~K \citep{Penteado2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-209: At smaller scales, \cite{zhao2024 report the H$_2$CO kinetic temperature to be 103~K, so we are likely seeing the hot core smoothed out, and diluting the temperature with the cooler, extended gas.}
elements.sent-2411-210: As CH$_3$CCH is seen as an envelope molecule, we tried simulating a cloud-edge environment with an $A_V$ of 1 (Fig.~\ref{fig:2stage , bottom).}
elements.sent-2411-211: In this scenario, the chemical evolution is influenced by the external interstellar radiation field (e.g. \citealt{spezzano2016, Kalvans2021 ).}
elements.sent-2411-212: The abundances for both components are reached at a few hundreds of years after the cold cloud stage.
elements.sent-2411-213: At 10$^4$ years, there is a sharp decrease in abundance.
elements.sent-2411-214: The main production route (Fig.~\ref{fig:proddestch3cch ), from about 20 years on, is the grain surface hydrogenation: \begin{equation}\mathrm{gr(H)} + \mathrm{gr(CH_2CCH)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CCH}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Prior, it's the grain surface reaction: \begin{equation}\mathrm{gr(CH)} + \mathrm{gr(C_2H_3)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CCH}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-216: Relatively, the grain surface production is largely dominant over gas phase production (as in \citealt{hickson2016, ines2018, calcutt2019 ).}
elements.sent-2411-217: This is not consistent with CH$_3$CCH being formed in a WCCC scenario.
elements.sent-2411-218: Once the abundance peaks, the main destruction route is photodissociation to H and CH$_2$CCH -- assumed to be influenced by the low $A_V$ environment.
elements.sent-2411-219: However, DR21(OH) is located within a clustered region, and we do not know if the extent of CH$_3$CCH is truly on the envelope edge.
elements.sent-2411-220: Values of $A_V$ up to $\sim$5 still worked in our model if the UV field in DR21 is larger than unity.
elements.sent-2411-221: However, modelling CH$_3$CCH with a higher $A_V$ corresponding to cloud core conditions (as in H$_2$CO, $A_V$=50) failed to reproduce the observed abundances at any value of the UV field.
elements.sent-2411-222: In this case, a shock -- a sharp spike in temperature and density -- was needed to reproduce the CH$_3$CCH abundances.
elements.sent-2411-223: This model was also introduced for CH$_3$OH, and is described next.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[trim = 1cm 0 1cm 0, clip=True, scale=0.5]{figures/2stage_edit.png} \caption{The two-stage model for H$_2$CO (top) and CH$_3$CCH (bottom). The 10~K cold cloud stage (left) is followed by a warm up stage where the LTE components are separated based on the temperatures and densities observed. The time axis represents the age of each component independently. The components of each model are in dotted, dashed, or solid lines, where the coloured lines are the model abundances and the black lines are the observed abundances calculated from the averages in Table~\ref{tab:ltemodel}.} \label{fig:2stage}
elements.sent-2411-224: For CH$_3$OH, the warm-up model, with any value of $A_V$ or $n_{\mathrm{p-H_2 }$, did not reproduce the abundances.}
elements.sent-2411-225: We then used the approach as in \cite{freeman2023 and simulated a shock passing through the cloud.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Shocks, which can be produced by outflows such as the ones in our source, can liberate grain species into the gas phase causing a sharp increase in gas phase abundances (\citealt{herbst2009, palau2017 ).}
elements.sent-2411-227: While we cannot model a shock directly, we simulate shock-induced, non-thermal desorption by implementing a three stage model: a cold quiescent cloud, a `shock' with a sharp increase in temperature and density, and a post-shock phase where the gas settles to the observed conditions.
elements.sent-2411-228: The cold cloud is the same as the previous models.
elements.sent-2411-229: In the shock stage, the gas temperature and density evolve as in \citet{palau2017 for IRAS 20126 (see their Fig. 5, top).}
elements.sent-2411-230: This assumes a C-type shock as in \cite{jimenezserra2008 with a shock velocity of 40~km s$^{-1}$ and a pre-shock density of $10^4$~cm$^{-3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-231: This lasts for 10$^4$ years, during which the temperature sharply increased to 2500~K before decreasing, while the density slowly increased from $10^4$ to $8.2\times10^4$~cm$^{-3}$, where it stabilised.
elements.sent-2411-232: We fix the dust temperature to 80~K.
elements.sent-2411-233: Then, in the post-shock phase, we return the temperature to those observed from the LTE model (as used in the warm-up stage above), set the density at 10$^5$ cm$^{-2}$ and allowed the cloud to evolve for a final $10^6$ years.
elements.sent-2411-234: For all five components, this reproduced the abundances (Fig.~\ref{fig:shock ).}
elements.sent-2411-235: In component 2, the warm 80~K region, the main production route is the gas phase reaction (Fig.~\ref{fig:proddestch3oh2 ): \begin{equation}\mathrm{CH_3OCH_3} + \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3OH} + \mathrm{C_2H_2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-236: After 10$^5$ years the dominant mechanism sharply changes to: \begin{equation}\mathrm{CH_3OCH_4^+} + \mathrm{e^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3} + \mathrm{CH_3OH}. \label{eq:dissrecomb}\end{equation} We assume component 1 is similar from their physical parameters (Table~\ref{tab:ltemodel ).}
elements.sent-2411-237: Due to their higher temperature compared to the other components, we assume these are still in a shock stage in Fig.~\ref{fig:shock , where their abundances match the model after less than 10 years.}
elements.sent-2411-238: For both the cold and narrow component 4 and the cold and broad component 5, grain surface production dominates through: \begin{equation}\mathrm{gr(H)} + \mathrm{gr(CH_3O)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3OH},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\mathrm{gr(H)} + \mathrm{gr(CH_2OH)} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3OH}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-239: This is dominant over 10$^6$ years in component 4.
elements.sent-2411-240: For component 5, after 10$^4$ years, the gas phase dissociative recombination (Eq.~\ref{eq:dissrecomb ) dominates.}
elements.sent-2411-241: In the model, we assume these colder components are in the post-shock stage in Fig.~\ref{fig:shock .}
elements.sent-2411-242: Their model abundances are slightly overproduced until about 10$^5$ years, where they sharply decrease.
elements.sent-2411-243: In the cold components, it is clear that some non-thermal desorption mechanism after grain surface production is key to producing the gas phase abundances.
elements.sent-2411-244: For all components, the main destruction route is adsorption to the grain surface until about 10$^5$ years.
elements.sent-2411-245: After that, in all components, there are contributions from gas phase ion-molecule reactions.
elements.sent-2411-246: To see if the timescales are physically realistic, we look at the outflow.
elements.sent-2411-247: For a flow moving at 7 km s$^{-1}$ (from component N44 5, Table~\ref{tab:ltemodel ), to span 10$^{\prime\prime}$ (Fig.~\ref{fig:n44ntot , \citealt{zapata2012 Fig. 2), it would need to be 1.6 $\times$ 10$^4$ years old.}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: This is less than the time it takes for all five components to match model and observed abundances in the post-shock stage.
elements.sent-2411-249: We found that in higher density models the same evolution proceeded on a faster timescale.
elements.sent-2411-250: We tried to use the RADEX densities in the shock model as we had for the warm-up models to reconcile the results of our different analyses.
elements.sent-2411-251: If the RADEX densities were used in the post-shock stage, the abundances would match at a few 10$^3$ years for components 3, 4, and 5.
elements.sent-2411-252: However, there would be a sharp density jump from the shock stage to the post-shock stage in this scenario, for which we have no explanation.
elements.sent-2411-253: We tried using the RADEX densities in the shock stage, and the CH$_3$OH was destroyed quickly.
elements.sent-2411-254: However, along with the abundances not matching, we have no basis for such high densities in the shock stage as we use a previously parameterised shock.
elements.sent-2411-255: Different type shocks have been explored for example in L1157 \citep{james2020 , but it is out of the scope of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-256: We can only conclude that CH$_3$OH need some form of non-thermal desorption to produce the observed abundances.
elements.sent-2411-257: We can speculate on possible sources powering non-thermal desorption in DR21(OH).
elements.sent-2411-258: There are numerous outflows seen emanating from MM1 and MM2, including the previously discussed molecular blue-shifted outflow, and the E-W CH$_3$OH maser outflows which contain other species such as H$_2$CO and H$^{13}$CO$^+$ (\citealt{zapata2012, OrozcoAguilera2019 ) as well as thermal CH$_3$OH emission.}
elements.sent-2411-259: A N-S H$_2$CS outflow is seen from MM1 with rotation temperatures of 40-60~K \citep{minh2011 , where lines of CH$_3$OH are also detected \citep{minh2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-260: They suggest CH$_3$OH could arise from the same mechanism as H$_2$CS: non-thermal desorption from outflows interacting with the surrounding gas.
elements.sent-2411-261: While we cannot discern the exact sources of the non-thermal desorption in the CH$_3$OH components, the clustered cores of MM1 and MM2 are evidently producing multiple sites of turbulent gas.
elements.sent-2411-262: Further modelling of higher spatial resolution data is needed to determine the structure of DR21(OH) and connect it to the potential molecular formation routes.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[trim = 1cm 0.1cm 1cm 0, clip=True, scale=0.5]{figures/shock_low_density_edit.png} \caption{The three-stage model for CH$_3$OH, simulating a shock. The 10~K cold cloud stage (left) is followed by a sharp increase in density and temperature to simulate a shock (middle) and then a post-shock stage (right), where the gas settles to the LTE conditions observed. The time axis represents the age of each component independently. The LTE components are separated based on their temperatures and densities. The components of each model are in dotted, dashed, or solid lines, where the coloured lines are the model abundances and the black lines are the observed abundances calculated from the averages in Table~\ref{tab:ltemodel}. As C1 and C2 are warmer, we suggest they are still in the shock stage, whereas the cooler C3, C4, and C5 are in a post-shock state.} \label{fig:shock}
elements.sent-2411-263: We presented IRAM and GBT observations mapping DR21(OH) at 10$\arcsec$ resolution.
elements.sent-2411-264: We used CH$_3$CCH, CH$_3$OH and H$_2$CO emission lines to determine the physical parameters of the different gaseous environments in the region, connecting the core scales to the larger clump and filaments scales.
elements.sent-2411-266: DR21(OH) main, or N44, is found to have a $\sim$80~K, and $\sim$30~K components near both MM1 and MM2, interpreted as the warmer inner core and the cooler envelope.
elements.sent-2411-267: H$_2$CO and CH$_3$OH also show a broad, blue-shifted outflow component that is not associated with the maser outflows of this region.
elements.sent-2411-268: For CH$_3$CCH and CH$_3$OH especially, grain surface production is important to reproducing their gas phase abundances.
elements.sent-2411-269: The cold components seen are likely non-LTE, and represent sub-thermally excited gas.
elements.sent-2411-270: With a sophisticated LTE model we are able to use large-scale single-dish data to disentangle different temperature and velocity components of star-forming clumps and cores.
elements.sent-2411-271: This is useful even for complicated species such as CH$_3$OH, as we are able to apply the model over the whole map along with targeted use of the more computationally expensive RADEX.
elements.sent-2411-272: The LTE model allows us to confidently determine the abundances of each of these species, essential for determining the chemical evolution of the region.
elements.sent-2411-273: This work is based on observations carried out under project numbers 021-20 and 122-20 with the 30m telescope.

elements.sent-2411-279: PF and RP would like to thank K.. Qiu and Y.
elements.sent-2411-280: Cao for providing the H$_2$ columnn density maps.

The components are indicated by dashed or dotted coloured lines: the dashed orange and purple are the warm components of each species, the dotted yellow and blue are the cold components in CH$_3$OH, and the dot-dashed teal is the broad outflow component. The upper energy level of each transition line is noted on the plot, with its frequency at the source $v_{\mathrm{lsr}}$ shown as the vertical grey line.} \label{fig:n44lines}

elements.section-2411-007: Production and destruction routes from the NAUTILUS model
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[trim= 0 1cm 0 2cm, clip=True, scale=0.5]{figures/prod_dest_H2CO_C1_C2.pdf} \caption{The production (top) and destruction (bottom) routes for H$_2$CO, components 1 and 2. The reactions are described in each legend. The designation gr() stands for a grain surface process.} \label{fig:proddesth2co1}
